result.markdown: --- title: 'Do Androids Dream of Riding the Iron Horse?' date: '2023-07-30' categories: ['pyspark', 'llm', 'data-analytics', 'trains', 'google-search-api'] description: 'Exploring lists of trains with AI.' execute: message: false warning: false editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ![Train](./train.jpg){#fig-hero fig-align="left"} Photo by JK on Unsplash ## 0. Executive Summary In which we try to perform the sacred task of Exploratory Data Analysis using a combination of Spark and LLM's through a new stack launched by DataBricks called PySpark. We will use this to try and visualize the top 50 longest train services in the world using PySpark, Google Search and GPT 4. You will see us fail. As always, that's a teachable moment. ## 1. Introduction [^1] Let me first caveat this: I'm more of a planes person than a train person. Aviation analytics, particularly with open-source data is such a vast topic that I can spend days talking about it. I do just that, in fact, 9 AM to 6PM, five days a week, sometimes more; it's my job after all. Which is why I'm not going to talk about planes here. Instead, let's talk about trains. Trains are all about _slow_ travel, about watching the world go by idly through various landscapes amidst banter and making new friends. I lived next to trains, both in Hyderabad in my childhood, and later in Singapore in my adult life, at least until they stopped plying in 2011. Indeed, it's been a regret of mine that I couldn't get to travel on some routes I've always romanticised before they shut them down. All good reasons why it's fun to day-dream which trains to take, and for how long. If we were to day-dream, why not consider the longest passenger train services in the world? Let's use AI for this crucial task. ## 2. PySpark AI ### 2.1 Big Data Meets LLM's Databricks announced last month that they were launching a new SDK to "enrich" your Spark experience through English language queries. So instead of writing in SQL or PySpark, or indeed, getting GitHub Copilot to suggest queries in SQL or PySpark, you could now write directly in English. By connecting Google search API, Langchain and GPT 4 together, you could ask queries in English to get pie charts and such. The stated premise is compelling. By typing just this [^2] ::: {.cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import sys from pyspark_ai import SparkAI import os import openai spark_ai = SparkAI() spark_ai.activate() # auto_df = spark_ai.create_df("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Longest_train_services") # trains_df = spark_ai.create_df("Top 50 Longest Train Services in the World") # trains_df.show(100, True) ``` ::: Search Engine Setup --- [^1]: I originally titled this piece _Do Androids Dream of Riding the Iron Rooster?_ as a tip o' hat to both Philip K Dick's _Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?_ and Paul Theroux's 1988 travelogue, _Riding the Iron Rooster_ . Seems like the term "iron rooster" (鐵公雞) in Chinese translates to a "stingy person". Because the then Xinjiang government was stingy in keeping up the then longest train in China, Peking to Urumqi, they used to call specifically _that_ train as a [鐵公雞](https://keywords.oxus.net/archives/2005/06/20/fire-carriage/ ) Instead, per Claude, 'iron horse' (铁马, _tiě mǎ_) is a more common metaphor for 'train'. For example: 铁马在轨道上奔腾而过 (_Tiě mǎ zài guǐdào shàng bēnténg ér guò_): The iron horse galloped past on the tracks. [^2]: You will need to sign up for keys from OpenAI and Google. 1. Sign up for OpenAI keys through [these steps](https://www.windowscentral.com/software-apps/how-to-get-an-openai-api-key). 2. Sign up for a Google Search API following [these steps](https://stackoverflow.com/a/62037708) 3. Save those keys in your conda environment following [these steps](https://gist.github.com/metamutator/74d7a24d904c9bd5774ce3c380434d73) Only _then_ will the code work as-is
